channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: El Boletín newsletter: Why Latin America loves to hate Argentina
item.description: Alessandro Ford, a Latin America contributor, explains why the rivalry runs deeper than the game

item.title: Café Europa newsletter: Spain’s champions tell a bigger story
item.description: Michael Reid, our Spain correspondent, on why the football team embodies the best of the country

item.title: Lebanon’s president wants Donald Trump’s help to rebuild the country
item.description: Joseph Aoun, who meets Mr Trump on July 21st, must quash Hizbullah, repair war damage and fix the economy

item.title: The surprising benefits of red light
item.description: Better skin, faster wound healing and reduced vision loss are on the cards

item.title: Don’t blame supermarkets for your expensive groceries
item.description: Saying greedy shops have caused rising food prices is populist slop

item.title: Why criticism of the Qing dynasty scares the Communist Party
item.description: It risks inflaming ethnic tensions and undermining territorial claims

item.title: Ukraine’s top general could be on the way out
item.description: Firing the reformist defence minister has plunged Volodymyr Zelensky’s government into crisis

item.title: Brazil’s beloved payments system has drawn Donald Trump’s ire
item.description: But his tariff assault on Pix just makes Brazilians love it—and Lula—more

item.title: China’s mysterious new billionaires are conquering the world
item.description: And refusing to talk to the press

item.title: How India is weathering the energy shock
item.description: And why its troubles may not be over yet

item.title: The Iran war is America’s least popular since polls began
item.description: Support seldom rises for conflicts as they age

item.title: Closing Europe’s dangerous missile gap
item.description: European NATO powers scramble to acquire capabilities as America cuts its help

item.title: Can Spain’s teamwork overcome Lionel Messi’s genius?
item.description: The World Cup finalists are strikingly different teams

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Make America worse at sports
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our US editor, on why the fantasy of athletic greatness has become a plague on American life

item.title: The Climate Issue newsletter: The dangers of global dimming
item.description: Oliver Morton, our planetary affairs editor, reflects on a warming Earth

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: It’s too darn hot
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Should you microdose GLP-1 drugs for weight loss?
item.description: Possibly. But only under medical supervision

item.title: Boomers have the good life, but it could be longer
item.description: A single chart shows why they have missed out on extra years

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s testosterone-screening plan is unusual, not crazy
item.description: Soldiers seem to be at particular risk of hypogonadism

item.title: Russia is losing its grip on Crimea
item.description: Ukraine’s drone offensive is making it harder to supply and defend the vital peninsula

item.title: Cartoon: Continuing uncertainty over the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Israel’s future hangs in the balance
item.description: The country’s coming election may be its most important ever

item.title: America should stop making it so hard to have fun
item.description: Bureaucracy is nightlife’s toughest bouncer

item.title: China’s rulers have a woman problem
item.description: Antagonism between the sexes does nothing to address China’s demographic problems

item.title: A real but selective crackdown on Cambodia’s scam industry
item.description: Sanctions from America and pressure from China have forced a reckoning

item.title: China wants to end AI romances
item.description: They are having too much impact on young people’s lives

item.title: Lindsey Graham tried to save the wrong countries from Donald Trump
item.description: He should have put American democracy first

item.title: Democrats challenge a big Hollywood tie-up
item.description: A dozen states are suing to block Paramount’s merger with Warner Bros

item.title: Business
Politics
In praise of Scandinavia’s risky and dirty playgrounds
item.description: Kids stand to gain from a few bumps, bruises and mud-splatters

item.title: Sweden squashed migration. The populist right wants to go further
item.description: The Sweden Democrats could be heading for a bigger role in government

item.title: Opening the border between Spain and Gibraltar
item.description: The deal offers hope for a more pragmatic relationship between Britain and Europe

item.title: Why Berlin’s airport is still completely hopeless
item.description: Blame passengers, lobbying, history and geography

item.title: Europe seems set to ease its carbon pricing
item.description: Green goals are running into fears about competitiveness

item.title: How SK Hynix became the king of advanced memory chips
item.description: Its advantages will not protect it if demand falters

item.title: How to sell a kettle
item.description: Brand positioning reaches boiling point

item.title: A hit new show takes on polygamy in modern South Africa
item.description: “The Polygamist” is trashy yet perceptive about family, patriarchy and inequality

item.title: America’s other battle with Iran
item.description: The two countries are vying for influence in Iraq

item.title: The Panama Canal is growing more important
item.description: And the challenges to it are getting more acute

item.title: The biggest schools training America’s doctors are in the Caribbean
item.description: The industry has a seedy underbelly

item.title: An unreliable America is drawing Asia’s middle powers closer
item.description: A burst of Indian diplomacy exemplifies the trend

item.title: Sovereign AI, independent of America and China, is a pipe dream
item.description: But a degree of protection from coercion remains possible

item.title: Ukraine’s reformist defence minister is ousted
item.description: The generals pushed back at his efforts to modernise the army

item.title: Donald Trump’s transformation of the Department of Justice will be hard to undo
item.description: Political prosecutions are just part of a more radical reinvention

item.title: How to shrink the Fed’s $7trn balance-sheet
item.description: Kevin Warsh will struggle to reverse the effects of bond-buying

item.title: Donald Trump’s gutting of the Department of Justice
item.description: What the degraded institution means for America

item.title: It’s too darn hot. Blame global dimming
item.description: Earth is absorbing a lot more sunshine

item.title: Can Kevin Warsh’s Fed force 5 reimagine monetary policymaking?
item.description: The new chairman enlists heavy-hitters to fight a handful of gnarly problems

item.title: Donald Trump’s blind alley
item.description: America’s president looks bereft of good options for solving the stand-off in the Gulf

item.title: The rate at which Earth is absorbing energy is alarming scientists
item.description: Reflections on a warming planet

item.title: Earth is dimming
item.description: The planet is reflecting less light back into space. That is making the world hotter

item.title: How to make AI safe—and lessen dependence on America and China
item.description: State-backed efforts to catch up with frontier models are doomed

item.title: Eli Lilly is reinventing the pharma business
item.description: The world’s largest drugmaker is betting big on preventive medicines—and learning from big tech

item.title: China’s trade gap is narrowing. And other surprises
item.description: The world’s second-biggest economy is stumbling into fiscal austerity

item.title: Meet the committee to buy Europe
item.description: The continent’s dealmakers deserve much more attention

item.title: How to preserve Africa’s architectural gems
item.description: Development and restoration must go hand in hand

item.title: Xi Jinping expels another leader from his top team
item.description: He has set a record for booting out members of the Politburo

item.title: Argentina v England is one of the most intense rivalries in sport
item.description: The two countries have not played each other in over two decades

item.title: When China’s open-source AI is a trap
item.description: America’s quest for AI dominance is scary. China is not the solution

item.title: Cities are rethinking what happens after dark
item.description: Making it easier to party is becoming serious public policy

item.title: America’s Hormuz brinkmanship is worsening a global fuel crunch
item.description: Rising oil prices are only part of the problem

item.title: What investment gurus get wrong
item.description: To see a country’s financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.title: Haunted houses are in demand in Japan
item.description: What’s behind the growing desire for once-shunned “incident properties”?

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: Another looming atrocity in Sudan
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, argues that the conflict is not “forgotten” but “neglected”

item.title: Donald Trump has no good options for reopening the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Yet the stand-off has costs for cash-strapped Iran, too

item.title: Demis Hassabis has a plan to harness AI safely
item.description: The Google DeepMind co-founder sets out his vision in an exclusive interview

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Ukraine’s Game of Drones
item.description: Oliver Carroll, our Ukraine correspondent, assesses the performance of the country’s defence minister

item.title: The stubborn scarcity of female breadwinners
item.description: Wives who outearn their husbands remain rare in America

item.title: How high can Red Bull fly?
item.description: The world’s most formidable marketing machine faces growing threats

item.title: Ukraine’s reforming defence minister is under fire
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov has been trying to reform the army. The generals are pushing back

item.title: A squeezed China is trying to wring more from its state assets
item.description: Its latest campaign won’t solve its debt woes, but inches in the right direction

item.title: Is Aung San Suu Kyi dead?
item.description: Myanmar’s jailed leader has not been seen since 2022

item.title: America’s other elections problem
item.description: No other rich democracy hands as many decisions directly to its voters

item.title: Lindsey Graham represented the arc of his party
item.description: South Carolina’s senior senator is dead at 71

item.title: An incomplete list of falling objects in India
item.description: Prosperity and infrastructure development have not come with improved safety

item.title: The Ebola epidemic is getting out of control
item.description: It has spread to five provinces in Congo and may soon reach South Sudan

item.title: Storm clouds gather over America’s financial supremacy
item.description: Its payments firms may be the first casualties

item.title: China is dealing with its own manosphere
item.description: But online misogyny is only one corner of a much bigger problem

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The weirdness of American socialism
item.description: John Prideaux, our executive editor, on the rise of a new political class from the activist left

item.title: How to train for a heatwave
item.description: A nice, hot bath can go a long way

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The man who would change Russia
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Where is the home of football?
item.description: England invented the sport. But its modern centre of gravity lies elsewhere

item.title: How dementia is being defeated
item.description: Incidence among the elderly is falling fast

item.title: Who is capable of evil?
item.description: Stop lowering the age of criminal responsibility

item.title: A no-brainer for protecting your brain
item.description: One simple vaccination may dramatically reduce the risk of dementia

item.title: England needs fewer council homes, not more
item.description: Andy Burnham’s plan is no way to ease the housing crisis

item.title: Two cheers for Trump Accounts
item.description: The grubby scheme contains the seeds of a good idea

item.title: How South Korean Buddhists are trying to stay relevant
item.description: Life is suffering; the Buddha can help

item.title: Laos has big dreams of exporting energy
item.description: But its dams have flooded it with debt and other problems

item.title: China’s media gloat at Britain’s chaotic democracy
item.description: But Chinese viewers also love British comedy that laughs at politics

item.title: China releases a prominent Christian pastor
item.description: Jin Mingri formerly ran a large house church in Beijing

item.title: Child suspects are being prosecuted at younger ages
item.description: Some governments are lowering the age of criminal responsibility. That may backfire

item.title: The left’s common-sense problem on crime
item.description: Radical ideas may seriously harm the Democratic Party

item.title: Texas is waging a battle to stop “Islamification”
item.description: America’s biggest Republican state offers a playbook for a new identity politics

item.title: Why Donald Trump won’t let a doomed bill die
item.description: The SAVE Act is unlikely to pass Congress. Donald Trump may prefer it that way

item.title: Rise of the Gen-Z Luddite
item.description: Resistance to tech is brewing among digital natives

item.title: Cartoon: The real threat to global growth
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Business
Politics
Europe’s public broadcasters go from prime time to hard-to-find
item.description: Apathetic teenagers and culture warriors are hobbling venerable state media outlets

item.title: Poland and Ukraine have fallen out over history
item.description: The row could have lasting consequences for Europe’s security

item.title: The secret to good questions
item.description: Consideration, consistency and curveballs

item.title: Could a Chinese bike win the Tour de France?
item.description: The country’s manufacturers hope that high-performance models will improve their image

item.title: Ahmed al-Sharaa is quashing Syria’s vast captagon trade
item.description: But pills that cost cents to produce still sell for $25

item.title: Can Africa take the Asian path to growth?
item.description: Stalling agricultural productivity makes it look unlikely

item.title: Americans and Canadians are squabbling over mines
item.description: The frontier complicates disagreements between indigenous groups

item.title: Indian mangoes have been banned from Japan
item.description: It’s the latest sign of a widespread food-safety problem

item.title: China may struggle to fund Xi Jinping’s tech dreams
item.description: Even though capital markets are staging a comeback

item.title: An ageing society might not cost too much
item.description: A dystopian vision of the future may not come to pass

item.title: The man who would change Russia
item.description: A leading oligarch speaks out, warning of the looming disaster facing his country

item.title: NATO survives its bipolar summit
item.description: Allies breath a sigh of relief; Ukraine may even cheer

item.title: Elon Musk and the age of the corporate leviathan
item.description: For the world’s largest companies, the normal rules of corporate governance no longer apply

item.title: Is Donald Trump serious in declaring the ceasefire with Iran over?
item.description: Iran’s ultra-nationalist new leaders may welcome his bluster

item.title: Microsoft’s gaming strategy has misfired badly
item.description: A supply-chain crisis for Xbox couldn’t have come at a worse time

item.title: How the biggest trees survive droughts
item.description: Being tall would be a disadvantage, if not for some clever engineering

item.title: How to hide from killer drones
item.description: In the Ukraine war, anti-AI tactics are producing bizarre forms of camouflage

item.title: A statistician’s guide to Wimbledon
item.description: How to turn a tiny advantage into sporting glory

item.title: The future of chipmaking looks more like Manhattan than Silicon Valley
item.description: Constrained by physics and politics, chipmakers are building upwards

item.title: Russians are growing anxious and angry
item.description: The war has come home and is everyone’s problem

item.title: Brazilian cities are building vast surveillance networks
item.description: Can they combat the country’s biggest security threats?

item.title: Three ways the LNG market could crack before winter
item.description: War, weather and outages may still send gas prices soaring

item.title: Middle East Dispatch: Mourning and spectacle in Iran
item.description: The point of the mega-funeral is to demonstrate the power of the regime, writes Anton La Guardia, our diplomatic editor

item.title: Marine Le Pen is running for the French presidency
item.description: Despite being found guilty by the Paris court of appeal

item.title: AI has taken over the stock market. The bond market is next
item.description: Judging credit risk of the AI boom is difficult

item.title: Europe’s economy is a mess. Its stock markets are a steal
item.description: International investors still aren’t interested

item.title: What Graham Platner’s collapse means for the Democrats
item.description: The troubled Senate nominee has suspended his campaign in Maine

item.title: Rebuilding Syria will cost over $200bn. Bombs are not helping
item.description: Emmanuel Macron is greeted by explosions in Damascus

item.title: China’s semiconductor industry is racing to catch the West’s
item.description: But it is proving easier to design chips than to make them

item.title: Can cutting-edge semiconductors supercharge Japan?
item.description: Visions of a high-tech hub in Hokkaido hinge on a single government-backed firm

item.title: If ludicrous Trump flattery can save NATO, bring it on
item.description: The NATO chief Mark Rutte is quite annoying. It is in a worthy cause

item.title: The World Bank has ditched its climate targets
item.description: The rise and fall of the institution’s love for green growth

item.title: Beware the top-heavy economy
item.description: Supersize capital flows are reshaping business—and building risk

item.title: Mexico is placing an enormous bet on rail
item.description: Can it keep its plans on track?

item.title: A rare Chinese missile test unnerves Asian neighbours
item.description: China moves a step closer to having an operational “nuclear triad”

item.title: The world’s most, and least, liveable cities in 2026
item.description: See how 173 cities compare in EIU’s annual ranking

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Presidents and profits
item.description: Donald Trump’s legacy is still unfolding. “In the end he may be best known for reaping riches from the office only 45 men have had the honour to hold,” writes Charlotte Howard, our US editor

item.title: Women’s progress at work is stalling
item.description: Is the “Lean In” generation leaning out?

item.title: Has China obtained the world’s most important machine?
item.description: Its Dutch manufacturer is fighting to rebut American claims

item.title: Mehmet Oz, Donald Trump’s most effective TV star
item.description: The telegenic doctor enacting the president’s agenda with professionalism and zeal

item.title: Europe has promised cash for defence. It’s failing to provide it
item.description: Most Europeans want stronger armed forces, but are unwilling to pay for them

item.title: What Indians are reading, and what that reveals about the self
item.description: English-language books offer a glimpse of India’s hopes and dreams

item.title: Iran’s regime hopes Khamenei’s funeral will demonstrate its strength
item.description: It reveals how much it has already changed

item.title: How little exercise can you get away with?
item.description: Even tiny bursts of activity have measurable benefits

item.title: Who was behind those drone incursions over Europe?
item.description: A new report finds it was probably Russia

item.title: Turkey and Israel should trade energy, not insults
item.description: Both have much to gain from being less belligerent

item.title: NATO ponders how to defend Eastern Europe as America pulls back
item.description: German tanks are returning to a region they once razed

item.title: Donald Trump’s reflecting-pool debacle is a gift to America
item.description: What a fitting way to honour its birthday

item.title: Is The Economist always wrong?
item.description: We used artificial intelligence to test the accuracy of our forecasts

item.title: Business
Cartoon: A toast to America’s 250th birthday
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Politics
Happy birthday America, the ally Europe admires—questioningly
item.description: A tale of two continents

item.title: Ukraine is trying to threaten Vladimir Putin into peace talks
item.description: Its claims to be winning the drone war are partly propaganda and partly real

item.title: Indonesia gives its best-known entrepreneur a decade in jail
item.description: The technology giant he built is being yanked around by the state

item.title: The Toddyssey: an office epic that would make Homer proud
item.description: You could read Homer. You could see the film. Or you could just go to work

item.title: The next great Middle East rivalry
item.description: Turkey and Israel are natural allies and yet they are increasingly at odds

item.title: Africa’s new middle class is putting down roots in the suburbs
item.description: For a glimpse of the continent’s future, visit the edges of its burgeoning cities

item.title: Uganda’s volatile army chief goes after the press
item.description: Muhoozi Kainerugaba, the president’s son, looks increasingly like a despot-in-waiting

item.title: Africans are turning to Starlink
item.description: They are fed up with the continent’s terrible internet

item.title: Brazilians are going gaga for Chinese brands
item.description: They think China has better tech than America

item.title: The Caribbean has a problem with pesticides
item.description: It uses them more intensively than anywhere in the world

item.title: Promised reforms could change Cuba radically
item.description: But only if they actually go ahead

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why 2008 was a turning point in America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explains how the year laid the groundwork for today’s divisions

item.title: Venezuela’s earthquakes are partly America’s problem
item.description: The government’s response has been dire. Its patron has a duty to help

item.title: Venezuelans are furious with the American-backed regime
item.description: But the earthquakes could give it an excuse to keep putting off elections

item.title: India’s government overhauls a vast workfare programme
item.description: Critics say it has been gutted

item.title: Pakistan’s army chief battles with its imprisoned ex-prime minister
item.description: Which of Asim Munir or Imran Khan will prevail?

item.title: America should not imprison frontier AI
item.description: Fable is free. But the technology desperately needs better regulations

item.title: Is Germany’s government finally getting its act together?
item.description: A new set of reforms has lifted sagging spirits in Berlin

item.title: How much will a 4th of July cookout cost this year?
item.description: We calculate the rising price of Americans’ summer staples

item.title: Are stablecoins money?
item.description: Policymakers’ job is to make them safe as well as useful

item.title: Unpacking Venezuela’s peculiar debt restructuring
item.description: Hazy numbers and the absence of the IMF make for an unusual negotiation

item.title: America is anxious, and awesomely powerful
item.description: Restlessness is what prevents the republic from sinking into stagnation

item.title: We woz wrong about oil
item.description: The market bested us. But for The Economist, there is no shame in that

item.title: Show a liberal a Lime bike and he will show you his soul
item.description: Are the e-bikes a paragon of liberalism or an abomination?

item.title: Can Bending Spoons thrive as a listed company?
item.description: A rare Italian software star uses the private-equity playbook

item.title: Transforming the cradle of the Confederacy
item.description: Tourists are flocking to monuments on America’s racial history

item.title: Scientists take another step towards lab-made life
item.description: What do you call an organism with no evolutionary ancestors? SpudCell, apparently

item.title: China hushes up a plane crash in the heart of its capital
item.description: A light aircraft flew into Beijing’s tallest building. A news blackout followed

item.title: Europe’s airport queues are extremely long—and getting longer
item.description: The continent’s border-control system is out of control

item.title: Scientists can now study the event horizons of black holes
item.description: They make the universe’s most extreme gravitational laboratories

item.title: America is mighty—but becoming less dominant
item.description: Ahead of its 250th birthday, an empirical look shows the world’s leading country no longer stands so far apart

item.title: Allies learn how to bully America
item.description: Can Taiwan use its chipmaking industry to strong-arm Donald Trump?

item.title: A new Plaza Accord for global currencies wouldn’t work
item.description: The conditions and will for a deal are not what they were in 1985

item.title: Meeting Jordan Bardella, France’s possible president
item.description: The 30-year-old populist will run if Marine Le Pen is banned. Who is he?

item.title: Israel election poll tracker: will Binyamin Netanyahu hold on to power?
item.description: The Economist is tracking the contest for Israel’s Knesset

item.title: Donald Trump’s AI regime is opaque, unpredictable—and unsustainable
item.description: The administration now controls who gets access to the best models

item.title: Donald Trump’s hope for a new Middle East is premature
item.description: Recent attacks underscore the limits of diplomacy between Iran and America

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: South Africa is in denial about xenophobia
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, analyses the country’s shocking rise in anti-foreign sentiment

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The country with no army
item.description: A transatlantic rift is unsettling the land of fire and ice, writes Anton La Guardia, our diplomatic editor

item.title: El Boletín newsletter: An earthquake shakes Venezuela’s political reset
item.description: Our correspondent in Caracas reports on how disaster is testing an already fragile country

item.title: The Supreme Court leaves Donald Trump with plenty to celebrate
item.description: The justices handed the president some notable defeats. But their biggest decisions strengthened him

item.title: Turkey’s economic plan to win from the Iran war
item.description: The country is a case study in the spillovers of conflict—both positive and negative

item.title: Should every baby’s DNA be sequenced?
item.description: The genomic generation is on its way

item.title: America’s Balkan policy is all about gas
item.description: It is using its terminal in Krk to shoulder the green EU aside

item.title: How Americans see their country’s past, present and future
item.description: As America turns 250, our Economist/YouGov poll reveals an anxious and divided nation

item.title: Hong Kong, once a great place to raise and spend money, is halfway back
item.description: Tighter ties with the mainland have not always helped

item.title: The AI boom and geopolitics are rewiring Asia’s oceans
item.description: New cables between data centres are avoiding China and chokepoints

item.title: The rise of vibe lawyering
item.description: AI is emboldening people to represent themselves in court. The results are mixed

item.title: So far, so fast
item.description: Our report on gay marriage from 2014

item.title: Unfit
item.description: Our leader on Donald Trump’s response to a deadly far-right rally in Charlottesville in 2017

item.title: How will America respond to Venezuela’s deadly quakes?
item.description: The catastrophe could give the regime another excuse to put off elections

item.title: Big oil’s secretive trading arms are having an extraordinary year
item.description: Meet the corporate gamblers who never waste a good energy crisis

item.title: Why can’t India’s government build a decent website?
item.description: Clunky online experiences reveal the failures of Indian officialdom

item.title: China cracks down on rule-bending offshore investments
item.description: It wants mainland investors to bet on its own tech dreams, not America’s

item.title: Donald Trump is kicking out Chinese firms, but keeping their tech
item.description: China’s stranded assets are creating a new class of American green-tech investors

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The World Cup doesn’t understand America
item.description: John Prideaux, our executive editor, on why America is a spectacular place to host the football tournament

item.title: The Climate Issue newsletter: Europe can’t stand the heat
item.description: Oliver Morton, our planetary affairs editor, on why the continent is warming faster than any other—and adapting too slowly

item.title: Berlin is even worse equipped than Paris for Europe’s heatwave
item.description: Berliners gaze with dread at this weekend’s forecast of 40°C (104°F)

item.title: Is too much sleep as bad as too little?
item.description: The quest for the optimal amount of shut-eye

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Lessons from the Cuban revolution
item.description: In our bonus edition, Richard Cockett, a senior editor, explains why now is a timely moment to revisit the events that brought Fidel Castro to power

item.title: Drum Tower newsletter: Who is behind China’s latest AI breakthrough?
item.description: Corbin Duncan, our global correspondent, speaks to the Zhipu team on their technical progress, business model and the future of AI

item.title: Venezuela suffers its worst earthquake in a century
item.description: The impoverished country is struggling to cope with the damage from two nearly simultaneous shocks

item.title: Where will Europe’s heatwave be most deadly?
item.description: We analyse 854 cities to see which will be worst affected

item.title: AI models’ values are very different from most people’s
item.description: They are more secular and more liberal—unless they’re made in China

item.title: Latin America has turned Trumpy. That creates opportunities
item.description: Never before have the politics of the region shifted so quickly

item.title: University-for-all harms poor students the most
item.description: Time for sane standards in higher education

item.title: With Iran emboldened, its neighbours must put old divisions aside
item.description: With a common foe, they cannot afford petty feuds

item.title: Two state elections may break Malaysia’s ruling coalition
item.description: Anwar Ibrahim’s long-sought tenure as prime minister may be cut short

item.title: Asian governments are making children care for their parents
item.description: As populations age, the elderly are being neglected

item.title: Bangladesh’s main industry is battered by blackouts and rising costs
item.description: A crisis may loom

item.title: Students are doing worse than you think
item.description: Some at college or university are testing no better than ten-year-olds

item.title: J.D. Vance is heir to a more radical politics than Trumpism
item.description: …if, of course, he believes what he’s saying

item.title: America’s pro-Israel lobby considers next steps
item.description: Israel’s defenders are flailing at the White House and at the ballot box

item.title: Democrats and Republicans agree: AI is scary
item.description: It’s about the only thing that unites them

item.title: Business
Politics
The EU is just too damn slow
item.description: Why Brussels needs to pick up the pace

item.title: France’s south is testing out hard-right rule
item.description: The National Rally is mixing raw nativism with promises of fiscal liberalism

item.title: Why Germany’s cities are going broke
item.description: Bad news for residents, good news for the far right’s electoral chances

item.title: Peter Magyar is trying to fire up Hungarian democracy
item.description: A constitutional amendment would burn away the remnants of Viktor Orban’s regime

item.title: Teaching AI how people work is fraught with problems
item.description: Tacit knowledge is vital to many jobs

item.title: Why easyJet may be heading for a break-up
item.description: Its private-equity suitor may be looking to sell it for parts

item.title: UPS is losing ground to FedEx
item.description: The two logistics giants have similar strategies—and face a common threat

item.title: Medieval-style fortifications are back in the Sahel
item.description: Newly walled towns are a sign of shrivelling state authority

item.title: Two powerful earthquakes hit Venezuela
item.description: Such tightly sequenced quakes are rare

item.description: America’s disengagement means it is now the old continent’s conflict to manage

item.description: A Chicago suburb is paying reparations to African-Americans

item.title: Texas is America Inc’s new centre of gravity
item.description: Exxon’s reincorporation is one more feather in the state’s cowboy hat

item.title: Brazil’s high-tech voting system is losing voters’ trust
item.description: Blame social media, populist politicians and falling trust in institutions

item.title: India’s republic of uncles
item.description: A cockroach meme rattles the country’s gerontocrats

item.title: Pete Hegseth pulls his punches on China
item.description: America’s secretary of war meanwhile lectures his country’s allies

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The California outsider
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our executive editor and New York bureau chief, on the state’s bizarre gubernatorial race

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The new shape of war
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: Should you use a sleep tracker?
item.description: They are pretty accurate. But they could keep you up at night

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The most important wars forgotten by the West
item.description: Emma Irving, a news editor, examines the enduring impact of the opium wars

item.title: Mosquitoes seem to be getting over insect repellent
item.description: They learn to associate it with food

item.title: Why the world needs more franchises
item.description: From pizza to Pilates, franchises mint millionaires and make customers happy

item.title: The imperial vision of Ethiopia’s Abiy Ahmed
item.description: The prime minister’s ambitions threaten both his country and the Horn of Africa

item.title: llliberal leaders in mainland South-East Asia revamp their regimes
item.description: But they may struggle to solve their two biggest challenges

item.title: Indonesia’s erratic president grabs the country’s commodity exports
item.description: Facing a tumbling currency, Prabowo Subianto announces a vast new agency to sell resources to the world

item.title: Bowing to online fury, China’s censors ban a prizewinning film
item.description: Both nationalists and the manosphere resented its portrayal of their country

item.title: A coalmine explosion lays bare China’s two-speed economy
item.description: As its industries of the future take off, old ones still flout basic safety procedures

item.title: Are Angelenos angry enough to elect an insurgent as mayor?
item.description: Spencer Pratt tests the limits of grievance politics in a blue city

item.title: Why can’t Elon Musk do for politics what he’s done for industry?
item.description: So much for advancing civilisation

item.title: The refugees Donald Trump wants are white and middle-class
item.description: Afrikaners are welcome. Pretty much no one else is

item.title: Meet the Republicans defying Donald Trump
item.description: Conservatives with nothing left to lose are bucking the president

item.title: Cartoon: Uncertainty clouds the Iranian negotiations

Business
How the boomers screwed Europe
item.description: The huge rock ’n’ roll generation is sticking its few kids with the bill

item.title: Leo’s first encyclical attacks technological messianism
item.description: The Pope warns against replacing humans with AI, though he seems to use it himself

item.title: Europe’s superyacht-builders hit choppy waters
item.description: Fewer plutocrats want floating palaces

item.title: How should bosses talk about AI?
item.description: Employees are being asked to embrace a technology that causes fear

item.title: The Gulf war makes devastating oil spills more likely
item.description: It would also make them harder to handle

item.title: Congo’s response to Ebola is late and chaotic
item.description: It is hard to think of a more difficult place to stem an epidemic
